coredata.dc:title: Two-dimensional black phosphorus nanoflakes: A coreactant-free electrochemiluminescence luminophors for selective Pb2+ detection based on resonance energy transfer
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yanhu
Shi, Huihui
Zhang, Lina
Ge, Shenguang
Meiling, Xu
Wang, Xiao
Yu, Jinghua
coredata.dc:description: As a nondegradable environmental pollutant, lead ion (Pb2+) has been proven to be deleterious for environmental and health. Conveniently, quickly and accurately on-site detection of Pb2+ is of paramount importance. Herein, an electrochemiluminescence (ECL) assay protocol using two-dimensional black phosphorus (2D BP) nanoflakes as new ECL emitter for highly sensitive and selective trace Pb2+ was designed on the basis of Pb2+ induced ECL resonance energy transfer (ECL-RET) between 2D BP nanoflakes and Ag/AgCl nanocubes. Anodic green ECL emission of BP nanoflakes without any coreactants was achieved. It is noteworthy that the possible ECL mechanism and the influence of coreactants on the ECL behaviour of BP nanoflakes were further investigated. Benefitting from the well match between the ECL emission spectrum of BP nanoflakes (∼510 nm) and the absorption spectrum of Ag/AgCl nanocubes (200−300 nm and 400−700 nm), effective energy transfer yielded. The introduction of Pb2+ would lead to the detachment of Ag/AgCl nanocubes then result in an enhanced ECL emission. Based on this, the proposed method could accurately quantify the Pb2+ in the range from 0.5 pM to 5 nM, which exhibited comparative performance to previous work. Furthermore, this study presents the example of employing 2D BP nanoflakes as ECL emitters and constructing a coreactant-free ECL sensing platform, which might open up a promising route for the potential design and implement in clinical analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Black phosphorus
Electrochemiluminescence
Coreactant-free
Resonance energy transfer
Pb2+ sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analytical performance of this aptasensor toward Pb2+ detection

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structure characterization of the as-prepared BP nanoflakes: (A) SEM image; (B) TEM image, inset in (B) is the HRTEM image; (C) AFM image with the inset being the height profiles along the lines; (D) XRD patterns.

(A) Raman spectra, (B) UV–vis spectra and (C) XPS analysis of the as-prepared BP nanoflakes, inset in B is the photograph of the BP nanoflakes suspension without (left) and with (right) UV lamp illumination; (D) XPS spectra of P 2p including P 2p3/2 and P 2p1/2.
CV (A and B) and ECL-potential curves (C and D) of BP nanoflakes modified GCE in PBS.

ECL response of BP modified GCE (A) with and without TRPA in PBS, (B) with saturated O2 and N2 in PBS, (C) with and without GSH in PBS, (D) in PBS and in NMP, (E) in without, with Na2SO3 and NaHSO3 in PBS, and (D) in PBS with different pH values.

(A) The possible luminescence mechanism of the coreactant-free PB nanoflakes ECL system; (B) UV–vis spectra of the Ag/AgCl nanocubes (a) and ECL spectra of BP nanoflakes (b); (C) ECL dynamic curve of different modified electrodes: (a) BP nanoflakes modified GCE in 0.1 M PBS, (b) capture DNA and MCH decorated BP nanoflakes modified GCE in 0.1 M PBS, (c) Pb2+ aptamer labeled Ag/AgCl nanocubes bound to above electrode, (d) with the introduction of 1 pM Pb2+, (e) capture DNA and MCH decorated BP nanoflakes modified GCE in 0.1 M PBS in the presence of 1 pM Pb2+; (D) EIS responses of the electrode at different stages in PBS buffer (pH = 7.4) containing 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− as redox probe: (a) bare GCE, (b) BP nanoflakes modified GCE, (c) capture DNA and MCH decorated BP nanoflakes modified GCE in 0.1 M PBS, (d) aptamer labeled Ag/AgCl nanocubes bound to above electrode.

Standard ECL responses (A) and calibration plots (B) of the ECL system upon addition of various Pb2+ concentration, inset in (B) is the calibration plot of the ECL intensity vs. the logarithm of Pb2+ concentration; selectivity (C) and stability (D) of the developed ECL platform for Pb2+ (100 pM).
Comparison of main methods reported for the determination of Pb2+.

head.ce:title: Two-dimensional black phosphorus nanoflakes: A coreactant-free electrochemiluminescence luminophors for selective Pb2+ detection based on resonance energy transfer

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Collaborative Innovation Center of Technology and Equipment for Biological Diagnosis and Therapy in Universities of Shandong

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Collaborative Innovation Center of Technology and Equipment for Biological Diagnosis and Therapy in Universities of Shandong, Institute for Advanced Interdisciplinary Research, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a nondegradable environmental pollutant, lead ion (Pb2+) has been proven to be deleterious for environmental and health. Conveniently, quickly and accurately on-site detection of Pb2+ is of paramount importance. Herein, an electrochemiluminescence (ECL) assay protocol using two-dimensional black phosphorus (2D BP) nanoflakes as new ECL emitter for highly sensitive and selective trace Pb2+ was designed on the basis of Pb2+ induced ECL resonance energy transfer (ECL-RET) between 2D BP nanoflakes and Ag/AgCl nanocubes. Anodic green ECL emission of BP nanoflakes without any coreactants was achieved. It is noteworthy that the possible ECL mechanism and the influence of coreactants on the ECL behaviour of BP nanoflakes were further investigated. Benefitting from the well match between the ECL emission spectrum of BP nanoflakes (∼510 nm) and the absorption spectrum of Ag/AgCl nanocubes (200−300 nm and 400−700 nm), effective energy transfer yielded. The introduction of Pb2+ would lead to the detachment of Ag/AgCl nanocubes then result in an enhanced ECL emission. Based on this, the proposed method could accurately quantify the Pb2+ in the range from 0.5 pM to 5 nM, which exhibited comparative performance to previous work. Furthermore, this study presents the example of employing 2D BP nanoflakes as ECL emitters and constructing a coreactant-free ECL sensing platform, which might open up a promising route for the potential design and implement in clinical analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: As a nondegradable heavy metal ion, the widespread applications of lead ions (Pb2+) induced severe effects on the environment and human health (Yi and Zhang, 2020; Cui et al., 2015). Hence, for health and environmental safety, it is a great desire to fabricate a method for highly quantitative and sensitive detection of Pb2+. And atomic absorption spectrometry, mass spectrometry and atomic emission spectrometry have been demonstrated to be effective approaches for Pb2+ sensing with acceptable results, but suffer from sophisticated instruments and professional operation (Liu et al., 2016). To address such dilemmas, many other promising methods have been developed including Raman, fluorescence, chemiluminescence, electrochemistry and electrochemiluminescence (ECL) (Shi et al., 2016; Tang et al., 2013; Li et al., 2010; Zhang et al., 2011; Sun et al., 2018). Among these methods, ECL has achieved overwhelming success because of its high sensitivity, lower background, simplicity and wide dynamic range (Liu et al., 2015).

To implement a sensitive ECL platform, ECL luminophor plays a key role in such a system. Commonly, ruthenium(II) complexes, luminol, and quantum dots (QDs) are typical luminophores utilized in ECL (Liu et al., 2015). Despite their appealing ECL attributes, referred luminophores usually exhibit inherent vulnerability to high price, toxicity or low ECL efficiency (Xue et al., 2017). Thus, searching novel ECL emitters with high efficiency is a challenging and persistent theme in the realm of ECL. Over the last several decades, two-dimensional (2D) nanomaterials have captured the research community’s interest in view of their physiochemical properties and the possibility to motivate the future development of electronic/optoelectronic technologies (Tan et al., 2017a; Wang et al., 2018). As an emerging 2D nanomaterial, black phosphorus (BP) with tunable band gap, high carrier mobility, and ambipolar conduction ability, has ignited a fascinating of research since 2014 (Liu et al., 2018a; Wang and Yu, 2017; Wang et al., 2020). However, most research and applications of BP were confined to photocatalysis (Zhu et al., 2017a), electrocatalysis (Wu et al., 2018), photothermal therapy (Qiu et al., 2018), solar cells (Bai et al., 2016), or nanoelectronic devices (Ren et al., 2017). Besides, the BP possesses excellent biocompatibility and biodegradation that distinguished it from other 2D nanomaterials, which makes it promising for biomedical or analytical applications (Zhou et al., 2017). And BP QDs has been demonstrated to be a novel ECL coreactants or emitters in previous work (Zhang et al., 2018; Chen et al., 2020). In general, coreactants are necessary in common ECL system for achieving excellent ECL eimission. However, the introduction of coreactants would also affect the stability of ECL system further influence analytical accuracy. Therefore, it is very meaningful to investigate the ECL behaviour and mechanism of BP nanoflakes without any coreactants.

In addition, amplification strategies are necessary for conventional ECL assay system (Li et al., 2017). Thanks to the great advancement in nanotechnology and nanoscience, multifunctional nanomaterials, such as carbon nanomaterials, and metal nanomaterials, have been extensively used in ECL amplification strategy (Li et al., 2014; Zhou et al., 2018). And in recent years, catalytic reactions based on enzyme (Yuan et al., 2012), mimic enzyme (Zhang et al., 2014b; Zhou et al., 2013), or nanocatalyst (Xiong et al., 2016), provided another available way to further improve the sensitivity of ECL sensor. In addition to the above methods, employing DNA nanostructures amplification techniques based on polymerase chain reaction or formation of G-wire superstructure have also been demonstrated to be an efficient strategy for ECL sensing application (Wu et al., 2015). Despite the remarkable achievements, there is still high demand for developing novel signal amplification strategies with simplified procedure for ECL platforms. Recently, resonance energy transfer (RET) mechanism by leveraging of overlapped spectra of donors emission and acceptors absorption in close proximity has been proven to be an efficient approach for constructing sensitive ECL system (Feng et al., 2018; Huo et al., 2018). For example, Liu et al. used green novel BN QDs as ECL luminophores to design an ECL-RET/surface plasmon coupled sensing mode and to develop a truecolor ECL imaging strategy for DNA detection (Liu et al., 2018b). Sha′s group demonstrated an ECL-RET biosensor using g-C3N4 nanosheets as electron donor and MnO2 nanoparticles covered carboxylic multi-wall carbon nanotubes as acceptor for Concanavalin A detection (Sha et al., 2019). All those work offer an example for ultrasensitive detection of target analytes in diverse applications based on ECL-RET mode.

In this work, 2D BP nanoflakes were prepared and its anodic ECL emission behavior without any coreactants was investigated. And the possible mechanism was discussed. Benefitting from the leveraging of overlapped spectra of BP nanoflakes ECL emission (centered at 510 nm) and Ag/AgCl nanocubes absorption spectrum (200−300 nm and 400−700 nm), an ingenious ECL-RET strategy for ultrasensitive detection of Pb2+ was developed. The presence of Pb2+ in sample would induce the Ag/AgCl nanocubes detachment from the surface that terminated the ECL-RET between BP nanoflakes and Ag/AgCl nanocubes leading to an enhanced ECL emission. Based on the signal changes, Pb2+ could be measured sensitively and accurately. This work provides a new insight into the properties of the BP, as well as would provide a new idea for the future development of BP-based ECL applications.

A facile top-down method based on liquid exfoliation was used to the synthesis of 2D BP nanoflakes (Zhu et al., 2017a). Briefly, 20 mL saturated NaOH solution in N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) was first prepared, and 20 mg bulk BP powders was then dissolved into the above solution. The resulting mixture was sonicated in an ice bathing with a sonic tip for 8 h at the power of 10 W, ultrasonic frequency 20 kHz working 3 s for each cycle with the interval of 3 s. Finally, the product was separated via centrifugation to remove the non-exfoliated bulk BP.

After above procedure, 5 mg amino polyethylene glycol (PEG-NH2) in 5 mL ethanol was added into 1 mL of as-prepared BP solution under continuously ultra-sonication (Sun et al., 2015). After 30 min ultra-sonication, another 4 h continuously shaken was performed. After that, excess PEG was isolated through centrifugation. Finally, the final PEG-modified BP nanoflakes sample was re-suspended in aqueous solution for further use.

Scheme S1 shows the processes for the construction of ECL-RET system toward Pb2+ sensing. 0.3 and 0.05 μm alumina slurry were first adopted to polish the bare glassy carbon electrode (GCE). After rinsing with absolute ethanol and deionized water, a mirror-like surface was obtained. After drying, 5 μL of the as-prepared amino functionalized BP nanoflakes suspension was introduced onto the GCE. Afterwards, 10 μL of glutaraldehyde (10 % wt%) solution was casted onto the modified GCE to active the amino groups for 2 h. After removing unbounded molecules, the electrode was immersed into 10 μM capture DNA solution and incubated for 3 h. Then, the electrode was dipped into 1 mM MCH solution for 60 min at room temperature to block the active surface of electrode, followed by rinsing with 10 mM Tris-HCl (pH 7.4) thoroughly. Subsequently, hybridization reaction was carried out by incubating with the modified electrode with Pb2+ aptamer labeled Ag/AgCl nanocubes suspension (details in Supplementary Information) for 2 h for the formation of DNA duplex between capture DNA and Pb2+ aptamer. Thereafter, physically absorbed species were removed with 10 mM Tris-HCl, the obtained modified electrode worked as working electrode was stored at 4 °C for further use.

To carry out the Pb2+ detection, the obtained electrode was immersed into standard Pb2+ solution for 1 h. After rinsed by 10 mM Tris-HCl, the ECL measurement was performed in phosphate buffered saline (PBS, 0.1 M, pH 7.4) with the scan range from 0 to 1.5 V at a scan rate of 100 mV s−1, and the voltage of the photomultiplier tube was set to 800 V. Based on the recorded ECL emission intensity, the Pb2+ could be quantified.

The BP nanoflakes were obtained by sonication-assisted exfoliation method from bulk crystals. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image in Fig. 1 A demonstrated a typical lamellar structure composed of relatively dense layers, whose lateral dimensions of hundreds of nanometers with smooth surfaces. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image (Fig. 1B) revealed the layered structure with the size distribution with the size ranging from 100 nm to 1 μm. High resolution TEM (HRTEM) image inset in Fig. 1B showed the distinct lattice spacing about 0.257 nm relevant to (111) plane of BP lattice parameters (Zhu et al., 2017b). Meanwhile, the atomic force microscopy (AFM) image (Fig. 1C) revealed that layered BP nanoflakes exfoliated from bulk BP with distinguishable edges had the average height of 7 nm, which was approximately 14 P atomic layers (Lei et al., 2016). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of the synthesized BP nanoflakes in Fig. 1D revealed characteristic diffraction peaks located at 16.92°, 26.53°, 34.18°, 35.04°, 52.3°, 56° and 56.88° indexed as the (020), (021), (040), (111), (060), (151) and (132) planes for the orthorhombic phase standard pattern (JCPDS No. 73-1358) (Wen et al., 2018). No additional peaks can be detected, which demonstrated the high purity of the BP nanoflakes.

Fig. 2 A exhibited the Raman spectra of the as-prepared BP nanoflakes. Three characterized peaks located at ∼362, ∼437, ∼466 cm−1 match the A g 1, B g 2, and A g 2 phonon modes of few-layer BP nanoflakes well, revealed the high crystallinity of the BP nanoflakes by mechanical exfoliation (Huang et al., 2016). It should be pointed out the fact that the Raman peaks of BP are layer dependent, and the Raman peaks shifted to higher wavenumbers with the exfoliation (Shi et al., 2018). And the observation of higher shift evidenced the reduction of thickness BP nanoflakes. The optical behavior of the as-prepared BP nanoflakes was investigated by UV–vis absorbance spectra as shown in Fig. 2B, and a broad absorption region from ultraviolet to near-infrared regions was observed, that offers a new opportunity for the efficient utilization of solar spectrum (Cai et al., 2017). Furthermore, the well dispersion of BP nanoflakes in NMP showed a light yellow color (left inset in Fig. 2B). Upon excitation with a UV lamp, the sample exhibited bright green emission (right inset in Fig. 2B). The chemical composition and states of the BP nanoflakes are identified by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Distinct characteristic peaks of C 1s and P 2p are presented in the full XPS scan spectrum (Fig. 2C). Two strong peaks located at 129.4 eV and 130.5 eV (Fig. 2D) belong to the P 2p3/2 and P 2p1/2 states (Wu et al., 2018), while a small peaks detected at 134.1 eV may be attributed to the phosphorous oxides (P x O y ) on the surfaces of BP nanoflakes (Wen et al., 2018).

The coreactant-free ECL reaction mechanism of the BP nanoflakes was revealed by a series of electrochemical testing. As presented in Fig. 3 A, obvious anodic and cathodic peaks were generated by injecting or extracting electrons during scanning ranging from -1.5 V to 1.5 V with a scanning rate of 100 mV/s, while only evident anodic ECL peaks were observed (Fig. 3C). As shown in Fig. 3B, a positive scanning starting at 0.0 V was linearly applied in a positive direction, and anodic current was generated with the onset potential around 0.9 V, and corresponding anodic ECL emission was observed (Fig. 3D), which could be assigned to positively charged layered BP generated by extracting electrons from the highest occupied molecular orbitals and emitted light (Tan et al., 2017b). All above results demonstrated that the BP nanoflakes based coreactant-free ECL system was anodic emission.

To explore the possible ECL mechanism of the prepared coreactant-free BP nanoflakes ECL system, the ECL response under different conditions were investigated. First, the ECL response of BP nanoflakes modified GCE in presence or absence of tripropylamine (TPRA) which usually used as coreactant in anodic ECL system. As curves displayed in Fig. 4 A, no obvious difference were observed for the two curves, suggesting that the presence of TPRA would not influence the ECL response in this system. The effect of dissolved O2 on ECL response was also tested, as shown in Fig. 4B, there was no distinction either in dissolved O2 and deoxygenated PBS. The result demonstrated the dissolved O2 would not affect the ECL emission. To eliminate the influence from the reactive oxygen species, glutathione (GSH) was used as scavenger of reactive oxygen species. ECL behaviors (Fig. 4C) showed that both of with or without GSH did not affect the ECL response, indicating that the reactive oxygen species generated on the surface of electrode did not afford assist for the ECL emission of the BP nanoflakes. The influence of the solvent on the ECL emission was also investigated (Fig. 4D). An obvious anodic ECL emission could be seen for the BP nanoflakes modified GCE in PBS (curve b), whereas in NMP a significant decrement was clearly observed (curve a), which demonstrated that the solvent played a crucial role in this ECL process. To gain insight into the role of OH− in the ECL mechanism of the BP nanoflakes, the ECL behavior in bare PBS, Na2SO3 and NaHSO3 were investigated (Fig. 4E). The ECL response shows no obvious in bare PBS and Na2SO3. While, with the introduction of NaHSO3, the ECL emission decreased dramatically. Meanwhile, along with the pH value increased from 3 to 9, an enhancement in the ECL emission intensity was obtained (Fig. 4F). The result demonstrates the existence of OH- is responsible for the ECL emission in this system.

Based on previously discussion, the possible ECL emission mechanism for the proposed coreactant-free BP nanoflakes ECL system was speculated (Fig. 5 A). The electrochemical oxidation of BP nanoflakes occurred once positive potential applied and generated BP
+radicals (eqs 1). The generated BP
+ subsequently reacted with OH− to produce BP
(eqs 2). Then the excited-state was produced via the electron-transfer annihilation between BP
and BP
+ (eqs 3). Finally, a strong green ECL emission was obtained along with the excited state back to the ground state that releases energy (eqs 4) (Cai et al., 2018). And the corresponding mechanism could be summarized in the following Eqs. 1–4:
(1)
B P -
e -
→
B P
. +
(2)
B P
∙ +
+
O H
-
→
B P
∙
+
H 2
O
(3)
B P
. +
+
B P
∙
→
B P
*
(4)
B P
*
→ B P + h v

Energy acceptor with an absorption band possessing a perfect spectral overlap with the ECL emission spectrum of the BP nanoflakes makes the basic and key precondition for constructing an effective ECL-RET platform (Feng et al., 2018). As shown in curve b in Fig. 5B, the BP nanoflakes modified GCE yielded a strong ECL emission centered at 510 nm. And the Ag/AgCl nanocubes displayed strong characteristic absorption in 200−300 nm and 400−700 nm (curve a in Fig. 5B) (Ai et al., 2013), had an obvious overlap with the ECL emission spectra of the layered BP perfectly, demonstrating an opportunity for ECL-RET between Ag/AgCl nanocubes and the BP nanoflakes ECL system.

To investigate the feasibility analysis of the as-constructed assay platform toward Pb2+ tracing, a control experiment to contrast the ECL responses under different conditions were carried out (Fig. 5C). When the applied potential ranged from 0 to 1.5 V, a remarkable anodic ECL emission has been observed (curve a). After the immobilization of capture DNA and MCH, the ECL emission declined obviously due to the steric hindrance of capture DNA and MCH that block the electron transfer between the electrode surface and BP nanoflakes (curve b). When Pb2+ aptamer labeled Ag/AgCl nanocubes were bounded to the electrode, the ECL signal decreased noticeably (curve c) owing to the strong ECL-RET between BP nanoflakes and Ag/AgCl nanocubes. However, an increase in the ECL intensity was observed accompanied by the addition of Pb2+, which was attributed to the release of Pb2+ aptamer labeled Ag/AgCl nanocubes bioconjunctions that terminated the ECL-RET between the BP nanoflakes and the Ag/AgCl nanocubes (curve d) (Peng et al., 2018). Moreover, the absence of Pb2+ aptamer labeled Ag/AgCl nanocubes result in failure capturing of Pb2+, this process cannot contribute to influence the ECL emission (curve e). Such results confirmed that the changed ECL emission was ascribed to the introduction of Pb2+ induced ECL-RET, thus the proposed method could be used for Pb2+ quantification, as expected.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed to investigate the resistance changes during whole modification processes (Fig. 5D). The equivalent circuit model (inset in Fig. 5D) is composed of solution resistance (Rs), the charge-transfer resistance (Rct), the Warburg impedance (Zw) and the double-layer capacitance (CPE). The diameter of a semicircle fitting the impedance data could reflect the electron-transfer resistance (R et) at the electrode/electrolyte interface (Senthilkumar et al., 2017). The Ret of the bare GCE was quite small due to the fast electrons transfer of the sensing interface (curve a), while Ret increased after the immobilization of BP nanoflakes on GCE (curve b), meaning that the interface charge transfer rate is restricted. The further dramatically increased impedance after the capture DNA anchored on the electrode surface indicated that the unfavorable environment for electron transfer (curve c). When the interface was incubated with Pb2+ aptamer labeled Ag/AgCl nanocubes nanocomposites, the Ret further increased, attributing to the hindrance the electron transfer at the electrode interface (curve d). These EIS result clearly confirmed the successfully construction of the aptasensor. And the electrical equivalent circuit was initiated to fit satisfactorily the data as given in Table S1 obtained over the measured frequency range.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of this aptasensor toward Pb2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: In addition, selectivity of the present method was investigated using common cations as interfering substance under the same conditions. As shown in Fig. 6C, negligible change in the ECL emission intensity was observed for interferences. Therefore, this result demonstrated the as-fabricated ECL aptasensor possessed good discrimination ability for Pb2+ discrimination against other environmentally relevant metal ions. As shown in Fig. 6D, the proposed ECL sensor presented a stable ECL emission in the presence of 100 pM Pb2+ over 10 cycles without any noticeable change, revealing a good stability and reliability of the proposed ECL sensor. Furthermore, intra- and inter-assay with five parallel tests or five separate electrodes were determined to investigate the reproducibility. And satisfactory relative standard deviation of 4.3 % and 5.1 % were obtained, suggesting an acceptable reproducibility. Finally, the practical applicability of the fabricated ECL aptasensor was studied by standard addition method through diluting Pb2+ standards in tap water and lake water. As Table S2 presented, this method demonstrated satisfactory recovery for these water samples ranged from 97 to 107 %, that provided reliability and feasibility for determining Pb2+ in real samples.

In this work, a coreactant-free ECL-RET strategy which exploited 2D BP nanoflakes as novel emitters and further implemented for quantitative detection of Pb2+ was proposed. The BP nanoflakes were prepared through a basic liquid exfoliation route and employed as new anodic ECL luminophores without any coreactants, and the corresponding possible ECL mechanisms were also proposed. Due to the well match between the absorption spectrum of Ag/AgCl nanocubes and the ECL emission spectrum of BP nanoflakes, the presence of Pb2+ could induce the competitive displacement process contributes to the ECL-RET switch between BP nanoflakes as the ECL donor and Ag/AgCl nanocubes as the ECL acceptor. And this method exhibited competitive performance compared with previous works, and offered a new inspiration to fabricate a high-performance sensing platform without complicated labeling process. Impressively, the concept of employing BP nanoflakes as ECL luminophores presented in this work not only guided the applications of BP nanoflakes in biosensors, but also proposed an efficient strategy to further promote ECL assay based on 2D nanomaterials. The proposed method might be expended for other target analytes quantification in real complex samples, and had a great potential to be adopted as a powerful tool integrated with microfluidic devices, microchips, and screen-printed electrodes for precision medicine.

Yanhu Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Huihui Shi: Investigation, Formal analysis. Lina Zhang: Resources, Visualization. Shenguang Ge: Writing - review & editing, Resources, Funding acquisition. Meiling Xu: Visualization, Data curation. Xiao Wang: Conceptualization, Supervision. Jinghua Yu: Funding acquisition, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we do not have any commercial or associative interest that represents a conflict of interest in connection with the work submitted.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financial supported by the Taishan Scholars Program (ts201712048), Case-by-Case Project for Top Outstanding Talents of Jinan, the project of “20 items of University” of Jinan (2018GXRC001) and National Natural Science Foundation of China (21874055).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jhazmat.2020.123601.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: [Ai et al, 2013]

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hierarchical porous AgCl@Ag hollow architectures: self-templating synthesis and highly enhanced visible light photocatalytic activity

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Au nanorod photosensitized La2Ti2O7 nanosteps: successive surface heterojunctions boosting visible to near-infrared photocatalytic H2 evolution

Synergistically mediated enhancement of cathodic and anodic electrochemiluminescence of graphene quantum dots through chemical and electrochemical reactions of coreactants. Chem. Sci. 9, 6080-6084.
